Okay, so the two things I'm gonna kind of go through a little bit with you guys. Breakdown is the first one here is the 10x event, which is 3 million in 90 minutes. And the second one was FHL, which was, I don't know exact numbers, it's like somewhere between 10 and 12, but it's a 10 plus million. And by the way, I want you to give a huge round of applause to the man who stepped on stage the second day who we had a bet ahead of time. I was like, I wonder if you can get an extra 50 people and he was and anyway, he ended up getting like, I don't know, two and a half, $3 million in extra cells by himself. So let's give him Ryan, huge round of applause. I'm never going to speak again. I'm just going to like intro and tell a story and then be like, Myron's up. It's gonna be amazing. So we're gonna go on the road together. It's gonna be sweet. Okay, so the biggest thing is I was breaking the stuff down. It's funny because like, I was like, what was the big thing? And there wasn't like a big like, here's the secret, you guys. It was like this really weird revert back to the core fundamentals that went into both of these. I had spoken to 10X the year prior. We'd done about a little under a million dollars in sales from there. So I was like going back to the exact same audience. I was like, I'm going back to the exact same audience, the exact same presentation and I'm increasing how much I'm selling the product for. And so there's a lot of like, how do I do that in a way? Because I was like, the goal was three million. I was like, can we get a thousand people at three grand? That'd be three million. So that was the goal we kind of went in there with. And so that was the result I was kind of promising them in that event. For Funhacking Live, the big promise, the big result we're trying to get people is the Two Comic Club, right? Like, what is the core result? And I think I had kind of gotten away from this in the last two or three years and just like, oh, I'm selling funnels and click funnels and my weird niche funnel, all these kind of things. And I was like, hey, what is the result? Why are they here? And the clearer you are on this piece of it, the easier a lot of the other stuff on the map out is going to be. Okay, I'll say character. Number two is what? Future-based cause. Base cause. Number three was the new opportunity. So I want to talk about this, because this is the key here. Now inside the new opportunity, there's basically two different ways that you present a new opportunity, right? There is the opportunity switch, and there's the opportunity stack. Okay? Now, every business should have one and only one opportunity switch. We're switching somebody from where they're at into the new thing. Okay? So everyone's here, they're all trying to get this result. Right now, they're in some vehicle, like going up this mountain, trying to get the result, and I'm coming to them saying, look, the opportunity you're trying to get this thing in right now, it sucks, it's horrible, you need to step out and get into my opportunity. And later on I'm going to talk about opportunity stacks, this is kind of the next piece of it when you're creating an offer, but the switch is like, is the core thing. Okay, so what are we switching people from? If you look at it, you come back down to, again, expert secrets 101, it's like there's three core markets, right? So the three desires are health, wealth, and relationships, okay? So you figure out like, where does my business fit into here? And then from there you go off and then there's this red ocean, right, of where there's a whole bunch of competition here that are all kind of fighting over the same fish, right? And then our job and our goal is then to come back and to build our blue ocean, which this is where we're going to be hanging out and selling our thing, right? So the red ocean is like the alternative. So if I want to help someone get money, they come over here and there's a whole bunch of different red oceans here. There's real estate, there's internet marketing, there's all this stuff and I'm creating my own blue ocean, right? So what's cool about going to this event was I was literally walking into the stadium, there's 9,000 people in it, there's a dozen or two dozen speakers and every speaker all has their own thing. They're all trying to get people the same results. And so what's cool about it is I was able to see very visually, okay, like who am I competing against? I'm seeing the way to get this result is blah and then someone else teaching the way to get this result is blah and someone's like, so everyone's got like their system, their process, their path to get the end result, right? And so I'm coming in there and I'm coming into the marketplace and my job when I'm coming to marketplace is basically is you're figuring out, you're identifying, this is the red ocean I'm competing against and you're kind of coming back and you're throwing rocks back at it, right? And so what was really cool, I think the best way to explain this, what was really cool about the event was just the fact that like I was able to see all the competitors, right? Like here's, if you came to the event, there's all these speakers lined up, every one of them's offering you different results to get you to that thing, right? And so because I was aware of that when I'm giving my presentation, I'm like, okay, Grant had someone there talking about like the sales scripts, someone else talking about hiring sales teams, someone else talking about all these different ways to get the big result, right? How to 10X your company in the next 12 months. And so I had to come in and basically throw rocks at all those ways and be like, look, I don't care how many sales people you hire, you're not going to 10X your company in the next 12 months with sales guys. I don't care how you tweak your sales script, not going to work. Like the only way to actually get this result that we're promising you, the only vehicle that makes any sense is mine, it's Funnels, right? And the last day comes back and it's like, remember this $50,000 thing, it's not 50 grand, it's only five. And they're like, oh, it's so, and then they all go by. Right? And so I've never done that like in a 90 minute presentation, but I was like, I really want to do that. So the next way is you're creating your presentation and next time you're doing a webinar, I think through that. I think that they've sat on 12 other webinars this week, all promising them people the same result. Now I have to be able to go and cast stones and plants like seeds of doubts in all these other ways. I'm not blatantly saying, did you hear so and so earlier speaking? He sucks. Onyx speaking tomorrow, don't listen to him. You don't cast stones like a jerk, but I'm placing seeds of doubt in every other thing. And if there's a seed of doubt, they're like, oh, should I do it? Should I not do it? One of the best stories of seeds of doubt. How many of you guys have a water filter thing that's like 5,000 bucks? Do you guys still sell those? You should buy his. Anyway, so I bought one for like five grand. I was all proud of it. I was excited. We got all set up and we're using it every single day for like two or three years. And then Stephen Larson started working for me. And Stephen worked for a company that had water coolers. And supposedly this dude at the water cooler, they built this thing, is like a NASA scientist, figured out that none of the other ones actually work because of some reason I can't remember why. And they had this patent technology that does something that actually switches every time so it actually works. And it's the only one on planet Earth that actually works. All the other ones only work for like three or four days and they stop working. And I have no idea if what he just said was true at all, right? No idea. But it just seated down in my mind. And for the next two months, every time I walk by my water cooler, I'm like, I don't know if it actually works or not. And I stopped using it. I literally stopped using it. And then I went and I bought, for another $5,000, I bought the one from Stephen because he told me it was worked. And I remember like the way we had it, we had like built into our house and I couldn't get out. So I literally, to get the old man out, had to cut the cord and like pull it out. And my wife was like, why did you cut the cord? Like we could have given that to someone. I was like, it doesn't even work. She's like, what do you mean you sold me on this? It's like three years ago, this was the greatest thing in the world. I was like, I don't think it works. But that seed of doubt made it so much I couldn't even try it anymore. So when I'm doing my presentation, I'm looking at, here's all the alternatives they have because the people have alternatives for every one of the things you guys sell, right? So I got to start placing seeds of doubt like crazy. If I'm Annie, I'm placing seeds of doubt with AA and like any other alternative option, for all of you guys, what are they at? And I don't think we think about it most of the time because we're not actually sitting in a room competing. Like as a wrestler I'm competing, I would walk in the room, I look at the chart, see who I was competing against, right? And I would go and watch the matches. Like my dad would be filming, everyone would be watching the matches. We knew how to compete and how to beat every single person when we came in the stadium to wrestle. But in business, I don't think most of us look at it that way. And I didn't until that moment when I'm sitting there, I'm like, oh my gosh, there are two days of people pitching him how to get that result. I had to prove them that all the ones they thought in the past were wrong and all the ones you're going to hear about in the future are also wrong. I got to place seeds of doubt in everything else and I have to prove that this is the only way to get the actual result we promised. Does that make sense? All right. So that's the first thing here. Okay. Next I want to talk about is, okay. So all of this we're creating an offer, right? You have like, all visualized like as a stack slide. Sometimes it's not a stack slide, but there's like an offer, right? You're creating, like this is the thing I'm actually selling, right? And so traditionally, like when I was looking back about this, like when you're selling something like usually the first thing in your stack is like the switch. Like here's what I'm switching. I'm switching you into funnels. I'm switching into my new opportunity. And then usually all the other pieces in here are opportunity stacks, right? Like, so if you look at mine, my first two things are like, I'm going to give you the funnel hacks training that show you how to use funnels and the software click funnels. So there's my funnel, there's my switch, right? Funnels are the only way to do it. So here's the training, the software boom. And then after that, I'm not switching them from opportunity, opportunity, opportunity. This is actually why for 10 years of my life, I never made more than $3 million a year. Because what I was doing is I was taking my audience and I was opportunity switching them over and over and over again. We would back in the day, hopefully now you guys were following back then. Dean probably was, dang it, you guys were crap. Anyway, so what happened is I would be all excited like Russell normally is. I'm like, oh my gosh, this is the greatest thing in the world. I get people excited about whatever the new opportunity was, right? And we switch people into microcontinuity. And every go excited for three months, but we do a launch. I saw all the money come in and then the money disappears. And I'm like, I need to make more money. So every three months, we do a new launch, right? So the next three months, putting it into the next thing. And I'm like, oh, I got a new opportunity for you guys. Switch over here. And then people are like, oh, OK. And they switch over here with me. And I'm like, oh, it was amazing. And three months later, I'm like, here's the end. Eventually people stop following you. Because every time I get into something, they move into the next thing, the next thing. And it's like, ah. If you look at, I honestly think outside of fact, the ClickFunnels is awesome software. I think the reason why we have grown so fast and consistently over the last three and a half years is because I've only switched everybody once, right? All of you guys have brought you into this is the funnel world. And I've never taken you out of it, right? Everything I do now is related to this. So that's the switch. Every business should have one and only one switch. If you have more than one switch, you're causing a schizophrenia to your audience, right? So we switch them in once. Next now is opportunity stacks. Now I've switched you into this thing. Now how do I pimp out the vehicle we're going on, right? So the next thing you need is I'm creating the offer now. It's like, OK, you got the funnel software. You got the funnel thing. What else do you need? Now we need to start opportunity stacking. Well, you need copy for the pages. So we have funnel scripts for anything. You need traffic. So you've got traffic secrets. And so all the rest of these things are opportunity stacks within your offer, right? And so that's kind of what we're doing. Now this is the biggest part I want to share with you guys. Because if you get this part, this is the key to how this happened and how this happened. And just kind of put in perspective, last year, Funnel Hacking Live, I mean, you guys were the one last year, 2017? In Dallas. So it was awesome. Yeah, it was really fun, right? Guess how much money we made at that event? Negative dollars. I still sold stuff. We sold everything. A bunch of things were there, but we didn't choreograph. We messed up the whole thing, right? This time, it was nuts, right? But it was about a couple things, about choreographing, which I'll talk about at the end of this. And the second thing was coming back to these core fundamentals of this, coming back to the fundamental of this, and then this next part. I did, in the Two Comic Club X coaching program, for those who are in here, I've done a few trainings on this. But when you're creating an offer, there's three things that are essential to making it work. How many of you guys have watched any of the, I might have talked about it in the Ten X Secrets thing, too. Anyway, and I'm podcasting, my brain's been focusing on this a lot over the last couple months. If you've been paying attention, you probably noticed me talk about it. There's three things. There's the hook, there's a story, and then the offer. OK, now what's interesting about this is, I think we spend a lot of time internally figuring out the offer. We're pretty good at figuring out stories, telling stories. This is a piece that we've been missing a lot, is what's the hook that gets somebody in? And the better your hook and your story are, these things, they're different, but they tie together. So the hook of the story, the better this piece is, the worse you can be at your actual offer. So I was doing, I think it was for Secret Smash Class. I had everybody, or not Secret Smash Class, for the Two Comic Club X coaching program. I had everybody who's going through that right now, which by the way, all you guys by tomorrow night are all going to be going through it with me. So you're all pre-prepared for that. But I had everybody go through, I had them do offer hacking. I was like, I want you guys to go and find offers and figure out for every offer, what's the hook and the story of the offer for the offer. And so actually Funnel Hacked someone in it. And the person I Funnel Hacked was Drew Manning. How many of you guys know who Drew Manning is? So Drew runs a company called Fit to Fat to Fit. What's cool about it, Natalie Hotzman was actually business partners with Drew in the business before the new one. And so she was able to share on Monday and Tuesday some of the backstory behind this, which was really cool. I didn't know all of it. So the backstory was Drew was a personal trainer, training people, making personal training income. But one of the biggest concerns people come is like, hey, Drew, you've always been ripped. I'm a fat guy. You cannot actually help me, right? I'm sure in the weight loss market, most people hear that. So Drew got tired of that. He's like, I'm going to prove these people that I can actually do it. And so this wasn't like a big media stunt or anything. He was just like, I'm going to do it. And he started talking about it just like his own personal people around him. He's like, I'm going to go from Fit to Fat to Fit. I'm going to gain a whole bunch of weight. And then I'm going to try to lose it all and to show people how it works. And so that was the hook. So he puts this out there. He starts gaining a bunch of weight. And without trying, without attempting, without calling media, without doing stuff, people found out about it. They started freaking out. And soon he was on Jay Leno. Have you guys seen the ads on Facebook? Like him on Jay Leno, like 300 pounds, all these things. And the hook was so good that everybody started talking about it. And it blew up this little business that was a personal training business. It's a huge thing. And then afterwards, then he lost it all. And so it went from Fit to Fat to Fit. And then that was hook and there was a story. And then behind that, he launched his whole brand. Now it's interesting, if you watch his offers right now, all he does is, and he's not like the most charismatic person. He's not like a great self, like any of that. But his hook and his story are so good. Even his offer sucks. Like it legitimately does. If you watch it, he gives you the hook. He tells you the story. You see him on Dr. Oz. Or not Dr. Oz. I looked at you and said Dr. Oz. You see him on Jay Leno. You see him all this stuff, all these things. And you're like, oh my gosh. And then he says, now I made a special offer for you guys. And it's about ketogenic diet. And I didn't even do a ketogenic diet back then. But if I had, it would have been really, really awesome. And then he's like, you're getting my ketogenic recipe book. And he gives a really bad offer. And he's become a multiple time two column club winner because of that. Because the hook and the story were so good, it didn't even matter if the offer was crappy. Imagine if you take the hook and the story and have an insane offer. Like boom. That's when it's crazy. And so as we're thinking about, I've been talking about this for the last couple of weeks. I keep thinking more and more about it. I look at Natalie Hodson. Natalie was personal trainer, fitness. She was doing stuff. And she was doing OK. And it wasn't until she had this insane hook and a story of, hey, I was a personal trainer. I was on a live video. And I peed my pants, which is a super vulnerable scary thing. When she decided to share that hook and that story then, boom. Million dollars in four months. Boom. Because the hook and the story was so intriguing and good. Like you have the right hook. People, they can't help but talk about it. And I think John, was it you tell me you were at church a little while ago? Maybe it was you or maybe it was someone who was at church. And they heard in the bathroom people talking about Natalie's or was it Dave? In the bathroom people talking about Natalie's course. When the hook and the story are right, the word of mouth will just spread because it's so good. Fit to fat to fit. I went to the bathroom. Dave, I shouldn't have told you guys that about Dave. This is awkward, Dave. He's a cross dresser at church. You think about the big brands throughout time that have done this well in our world. Robert Allen is a great example. Robert Allen's like no money down. I flip houses, blah, like 1,000 other real estate gurus. And then he came up with this hook. You guys can put me in any city around the world, drop me there without anything there. And within 48 hours I'll have a house that I bought with no money down. People are like, sure you can't do it. He's like, I'm going to actually do it. So he actually went and did it. And the news found out about it, covered it. Boom. Robert Allen's brand's done over a billion dollars in sales based on that hook and that story. Because he crafted and created the story. And somebody was like, well, I don't have a story yet. What's this like? Either there's a story that is just going to appear, which happens sometimes. Somebody's just figuring out a story from one of your customers, Alex D when he was here last time. He threw a box and he was asking me. He was like, hey, how would you make this headline better? And I'm like, you're hooking your story on very good. He's like, what hook and story should I use? I'm like, I don't know. Interview every single person who's ever made money with you. And the hook and the story is going to pop up. Like somewhere in there, it's going to be there. Or you need to go and craft and create this. It's worth it. You think about when we launch ClickFunnels, half of my initial thing was trying to figure out, what's the hook? What's the story? And the one that hit for us was I have this weird niche funnel, which is our neuropathy supplement funnel, that was making $17,000 a day. That story was the hook and the story that blew up ClickFunnels. But I had to go through all the stuff we were doing to find a story. I had done thousands of funnels ahead of time. But what was the story that was the right hook that's going to hook people in and pull them into your message? Right? You look at Drew, though. It wasn't something that he just made up off top of his head. Like he crafted it, right? I'm going to go from fit to fat to fit. So for you guys, I'd be thinking, how do I craft something that's going to draw people in? And recently, it's funny. I did this without knowing that I was doing this. It wasn't until Julie Storion later messaged me and she voxed me. He was like, you're using this in your new Book of Mormon podcast thing. And I was like, oh, I didn't even think about it. So some of you guys know I started a Mormon podcast, right? And I could have just been like, hey, guys, I'm going to go and do a podcast talking about my beliefs. And maybe three people to fall and listen, right? But instead, I crafted a hook and a story. Again, I wasn't planning. I didn't think through this. Looking back, I wished I would have, because it would have been really cool. But the hook and the story is like, look, I've been a Mormon my whole life. I've got Book of Mormons all over my house. And they sit there. They gather dust. We don't read them. It's not special to us anymore. I was like, I want to make it special for me and my family again. So I did. I went to eBay. I found one of the original $5,000 that was printed. Right now, on eBay, they're selling for anywhere from $1 million down to like $300,000 or $400,000. I found one that's cheaper. Still really, really expensive, though. And so I bought one of the original ones, because I want to make it special to me and my family again. And the whole podcast said, I'm going to be reading this and going through the journey with you about what I find and all the cool stuff that I'm going through on this journey with the original copy of the Book of Mormon. And so that hook now is like, got a whole bunch of people in. And then even on top of that, this again was not choreographed. Looking back now, it has been super, super effective. One of the guys in the Mormon Church early on, his name is Parley P. Pratt. And when someone gave him the Book of Mormon the first time, he got it. And he started reading it. And he got so excited, he couldn't put it down. He read the entire thing in a day. And so I heard that story again like two weeks ago. And I was like, I wonder when that day was. It'd be fun to do that with the original copy, right? And so I googled, and I couldn't find the date when he actually did it. But I did find it. His birthday was like four days later. And I was like, I should take work off that day. First time I take work off, like, I don't know, since I've been doing this stuff. Yeah. I'm taking the day off. And it's his birthday. So I'm going to do Parley P. Pratt day and try to read the entire original Book of Mormon in a day. And so I was so excited. I woke up that morning. I couldn't even sleep that night. I was so excited. I woke up at like 4 o'clock in the morning. I was freaking out. I did a Facebook live. I'm going to read the whole thing in a day. And that was it. Just my personal page. It wasn't anything planned. I was just excited. I did this whole thing. I'm going to read the whole thing in a day. And then I have a podcast. I'm talking about it. And that was it, right? And by the way, I spent 18 hours. I got 266 pages. And I did not finish it. It was, I don't know how that dude really did it. I think he lied. Because it was like, it was really hard. It was like midnight. I was like, my wife's like, are you going to finish this? I'm like, I think so. And like 15 minutes later, I couldn't even, like, all the words were mushing really weird. And I was like, I give up. I'm tapping out. So I didn't finish it. But I got about halfway through, which is really cool. I think I'm going to do another day and try to finish the whole thing. Because it was a really cool thing. But what's crazy about that, that one video on my fan page that did not have any ads, any boosts, anything, is that over, I think it was like seven or eight thousand views have come through that. Hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of people subscribe to my podcast. People have all sorts of faiths. I'm getting like, for me, it's like I want to use this as a tool to open up discussion with me about what I believe. And it's like, open up all these amazing conversations. But it was all based on a hook and a story, right? And so I just want all of you guys thinking through this more like, what's your hook and your story? And 10X, 100X, 1000X, my business, because the hook and the story is so solid. All right, so a couple of things about this now. So there's a hook and a story that comes into this, right, to the offer. But the next thing I really started figuring out, luckily, like these pieces all came in like two weeks before the 10X event, was that it's not just a hook and a story for the whole thing, like that's part of it. But then inside of this, every single piece of the actual offer needs a hook and a story as well. That's why Drew Manny's things such, his offer. He said, OK, you're going to get my Keto cookbook and my Keto recipes and my Keto grocery store list and whatever. And kind of went through really quick and he ended. I was like, man, the problem with that is like, all those things are amazing. Tell us the hook and the story behind that power of the offer. Two weeks before the 10X event, I did the webinar blueprint interview with Jason Flattle. And any of you guys listen to that? And there's one thing he said in there that was like gold for me. We were talking about how he creates offers. And he was like telling a story about, he was doing an e-commerce funnel training, something like that. And he was like, I had my funnels that I built and stuff like that, but I needed an offer. I needed to make my offer really really sexy. So I had to find people that have hooks and stories that I didn't really have one. He's like, so I'm searching for a hook and a story that I could leverage for my offer. And he's like, so I found Tre Llewell. And a lot of you guys know Tre. He's like, Tre right now has the highest grossing funnel inside of ClickFunnels. So he took all this information, he put it in chronological order, and then put it into a book, and started selling this book for $500 copy. It made millions of dollars with it, and then Jay Bram found out about it, sued the dude, got the rights back to the book, and then launched it as this book called the Mr. X book, right? One of the greatest marketing books of all time, it's $500 per copy, and it's amazing. And so there's the hook, right? And then I come into the story, like, now, Steven Larson is kind of like my Mr. X. Now here's the results of the people he's been personally coaching, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, right, so I do a hook story offer, pitching Steven first part of the offer. Then I transitioned to Julie, they just heard Julie speak, they know she's awesome, but I had to go back and pitch her, what was the hook and the story offer of Julie? Julie was just like you, 12 months ago, sitting in this thing, since then, she won a two comic clubs, she won a car, she won a two comic club for clients, she, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, I guess all her things, boom, and now she's here, and then went on to John to hold his whole thing with the making it rain, right, and then we went on to James, the systems, and Alex, and so I'd say hook, story, offer, each individual human in the human stack, and then all the other parts of the offer, and then boom, okay? So that's, again, it's just taking that little formula and plugging it in over and over and over and over and over again, and obviously it works really good. And then Myron came and slammed Dr. David Balsam, which is awesome. Any questions about that part at all? Okay, all right, so what we're doing now then, if there's no question about this, I wanna kinda go over some of the choreographing of both of these two events. As marketers, if you look at what we really do, we're choreographers, right? If you look at dances there, it's a choreographer who designs the whole thing and then you go out and you do the dance. Last year's Thunderhack, or, okay, so, excuse me, the first Thunderhacking live, we choreographed it, this is 2016? First one did in Vegas, anyone know that first one? Whatever the first one was, yeah. So we choreographed that, we sold the certification program, it did awesome. The next year we came back, Darren Stevens helped me choreographic, he was a choreographer, we launched the next version of the certification, we did over a million of that one, which was awesome. Then the next year, I choreographed it myself and I did a horrible job of choreographing and we made zero dollars, negative zero dollars, negative lots of dollars from the event, which was not awesome. And so this year we hired a professional choreographer to help us to choreograph the event, right? And so I'm gonna walk you through the choreographing of the event that we did that helped make this possible. And the same thing with the 10X event, so the 10X event, again, I'm coming to this event with 9,000 people who all heard me give the exact same presentation a year prior and I was selling the new product at $1,000 more expensive than they heard the year before. So I'm like, how do we choreograph this to get everybody to buy again? And so I'm gonna share a bunch of stuff that, how we did it, but there's stuff that ties back into whatever you do. If you're doing webinars, like a lot of these things you can choreograph back into your follow-up sequences and things like that. All right, so the 10X event, so how many of you guys do stage presenting selling? So one of the biggest things I found in my decade of doing stage presenting, I grew up in like a dang candy world and when someone was speaking they'd go to the offer, they'd always hand out order forms to everybody, right? And what you see is they get the most exciting part and they're like, hand out the forms and the forms start going here, this is ruffling the noise and everyone's getting them, they're handing them out and they're looking at the reading through it and they lose all connection with the speaker, right? During the most critical part of the pitch and I saw it over and over and over again and that's how they always did it, so that's how I did it. And then one time I saw, I can't remember who it was, but somebody didn't do that and it had just order forms on the back table and maybe people get up and run and I was like, it was like, when the first time that's like a legitimate table rush, people like going crazy and running to the table and afterwards I asked them, I'm like, why don't you have order forms? He's like, dude, that's the fastest way to kill yourselves. Everyone looks down and you lose the attention. So I've had a rule ever since then that we never had our order forms. And so the problem is that, so 10X event, for those who were there, those who saw it, it was in Mandalay Bay, which is a huge arena, there's a floor and then there's two levels of, it's like a basketball stadium, right? And so I'm like, there's no back of the room, there's no where to send people, like we had to send people like, go over here, go on the elevator to second floor and run around the whole building to section 118, that's where we're going. So I'm like, how are we gonna do, like we're gonna lose so many people in the hike over there. So we spent months on this choreographing it. And it's kind of funny looking back now because when the other speakers saw what we were doing, they're like, what are you doing? We explained it, they're like, you guys have really thought through this. I'm like, did you not? You're speaking for 9,000 people, like this could be the biggest payday of your life. They're like, oh no, it's funny, as Grant Cardone, they had an app and they wanted everyone, they just come and pull out their phone and just buy on the app. And I was like, no. And in fact, we were the only speaker, I refused to let them put my offer in the app. And they're like, no dude, this is the way it's gonna, I'm like, dude, if you put it in the app, I will lose all the money, no, I'm not allowed to be in the app. And so what happened, two things, number one, when all the other speakers like, pull out the app, they pull the app out and they try to work and guess what happened? Internet doesn't work very well in a huge stadium. So nobody could actually buy. Second off, there's no social proof. Ever since they're sitting in like, like you lose all the social proof is people running and fighting and like jump, like that's the magic, right? So we had to figure out like, how do we choreograph this in a way that we can get this into people's hands? And the second problem is like, in that room that big, like there's no pins. So we had to figure out a way to sneak attack in order for them to everybody's hands and to pin without them ever knowing it. So how do we do that? So this is what we kind of reverse engineered. So what we did is we decided to give everybody a gift. And so if you were there, the event, you saw this packet right here on the back was a big sticker, it said, it's gonna get out open until Russell presents. And so we're gonna hand these out to everyone's chairs, but then we found out like the week before that all the chairs are like movie style, movie chairs that pop up and like, this is gonna like fall off and, oh, so like Melanie and Dave and I had to go figure out like, how are we gonna solve this? So they went and bought these like click funnels bags. And then we put the packet in the bags and then we got permission to go in the night before with some of our team and like 40 temp workers. And we blanketed all 9,000 seats with a bag that had this thing in there, hanging out every single person's thing. And it took like three or four hours or? I would say like it was like two, three hours. Two, three hours. Then blank and height tires. And that's when the other speakers were like, what are you doing? Like we're getting ready to sell. They're like, you're gonna sell tomorrow. Like no, selling starts now. So we blanketed the whole room in these things, right? The other cool thing we did, Mike saw this. So we found out you can buy the TV spots on the room block. So if you turn on the TV in any of the room block hotels, there was me with my book like, my book is literally on fire. So people were watching me like, we're gonna try to buy 10 X socks. And the lady was like, she was like Jamaican or something. And she was like, I saw you. I'm like, what? She's like, your book is on fire. I'm like, it's literally on fire. It's amazing. So like people saw my face everywhere, right? So anyway, so we have this thing. It's all choreographed out there, right? And so when I get on stage, I start with my presentation. They get your distraction while they're doing the thing, making it disappear over here. So what they didn't know is that we sneaked attack the order form into all of their hands and the pen that we're sitting here secretly inside this thing. So I'm sure some people saw, but for the most part it's really quick. You kind of see it. You see the $11,000. But there's not a lot of detail. It's just kind of like a really quick thing. And that was it. And then I distract them as quick as I could to pull out the other stuff and focus here. But now the order form is there. Close to them, they all had a pen. So when I was able to sell now, I had the ability to do it really, really quickly. So that was the first part, how we choreographed that in. We got this thing. The next piece of it is like, I don't know if you guys do a lot of stage for them to do this, but I do this a lot. I learned from, who was it? Somebody taught me this. I can't remember who it was. But it was anchoring on a stage. So typically at Funhacking Live, when I speak, if you guys notice, there's the sales table back here. And then over here is the exits. Like whenever I'm talking about success and things like that, I'm always talking towards the side of the room. And I'm talking about failures, and things that I'm always anchoring away from the room, like this, that's happening for three and a half, four days when I'm doing that the whole time. The guy that, the person that I learned this from, I can't remember who it was, they talked about this. And I found it, I'll find it later. But Robin Williams was like the king of NLP and all sorts of stuff, right? So he would do a standup comedy act. He would stand up there and he'd get set a chair like it's on the side. And then he'd start telling a joke. And as soon as he got the punchline, the joke, he'd come over here and he'd lean on the chair right when people were laughing. And they'd go to the next joke, come back to him, some people would come and lean on the chair and they'd start laughing. He'd do it four or five times. Where all some people are subconsciously anchoring, like every time he touches the chairs when we laugh. And then he'd come back and like he'd be moving around and then he would just, every time we touched the chair, even like out of context, people would start laughing. And so he started doing that. And so there's a lot of cool things you can do like that subconsciously. And like, so that's what I typically do in an event. Now this was obviously hard because there was like a table here, there's a table here, there was a table like a thousand different tables. I couldn't do that as well. In fact, I'll talk about this a little bit more with the Funal Hacking Live, like the choreographing of getting people to understand how to buy. But for this event, how to get people to understand how to buy was super complicated. So twice at the presentation, I walked them through slide by slide, like so what you need to do right now, you're gonna pick up your packet and I held the packet up and it was pitch on slide. You're gonna pull out this form right here. You're gonna get the pen that I gave you right here and you're gonna fill out the entire form. After you fill out the form, you're gonna go run. If you're in this department, you're gonna run here. And if you're here, you're gonna run here. And like, walk it through. When you run, you're gonna find the people in my team. You're gonna hand them the form. Then they're gonna hand you back this other packet. Inside the packet, you're gonna rip it open and there's a golden ticket. When you get this golden ticket, you're gonna run up to section 118 with that golden ticket and you're gonna get a picture with me and it's gonna be the most amazing thing in the world, right? So that was kinda the thing, like twice during the presentation I walked them through exactly how to do it. Kind of another story that shows the power of this. AJ Brown, he has no AJ Brown. He was in my, a mastermind group I was in like a decade ago. He's in the stock trading market. I remember he had a really high bounce rate on his order forms and he was driving him crazy. So he like, one day he's like, I don't think people understand how to felt the order form. So he made a Camtasia video that went on the order form of him. They're like, hey, this is AJ again. You're here at the order form. You're ready to sign up. So I'm gonna show you exactly how to do it. The first thing you're gonna do, you're gonna fill in your name right here. Then you're gonna scroll down, you're gonna fill in your name and actually fill out the entire form with them, showing them how to do it. It was like the cheesiest, like goofiest, weirdest looking thing in the world. And it cut his bounce rate on the order form in half, double his conversion rates. And so ever since then, I'm always like, we have to show people how to consume, how to do the thing we need to do. If you watch my webinars, there's just two slides and I'm like, when you go here, this is what's gonna look like. And then you're gonna see this here and I want them to know exactly how to do it. Otherwise people get lost in the interim of it, right? Okay, so I walked into exactly how to do it. A couple other pieces that were really cool. We brought physical products that they could only get at the event. So they got a copy of the cookbook and the audio book, which gave people another reason to come up to the booth. And then the other piece, I learned from Brendan Bouchard. He did some of his events. He told me he basically, he was set up a photo booth next to the sales table. And part of his presentation is like, look, when I go to an event, I like getting a picture with whoever inspired me on stage, whoever, whatever it is. And then I print it out and put it on my wall and it reminds me every single day of like the commitment that I made here. I was like, all you guys who are here today, you've committed to make, to 10 extra business next 12 months. And so I actually want to take a picture with you personally. That way you can take that home and you can have it on your wall and you can remind yourself of doing it all the time. In fact, this house is gonna work. Here's a picture of me and Tony Robbins. This is when he wanted his picture with me. And I'm like, ha, ha, just kidding. This is right on picture with Tony. So I can remember this commitment I made to myself. And then I had a picture of me at the actual booth, like standing here, we did that before, like, I'm like, this is me at the booth right here in section 118. I'm gonna be up here in about five minutes. Everyone who buys is getting in line. You're gonna get a personal picture. We got a photographer up there, we're gonna get a professional picture. And this is a big thing, right? So what happened is, no matter where they went in the stadium to buy, they all had to go up there to get the physical product and get the picture, okay? And so it caused this huge rush of things and then people started running up there. And it was crazy, I forgot to offstage, I walked back and went to the bathroom and stuff and they walked me through the hallways up the back thing and then I came out to the spot and I opened up the doors and it was insane. There were five or six levels deep of people, both directions wrapping around the stadium as far as I could see it, people lined up to take pictures. And I was there for about four, four and a half hours doing picture after picture after picture. That's when somebody, you said yesterday, like someone came up to me and was like, this isn't fair, they shouldn't do pictures with everybody, they should do pictures with just the buyers. And I'm like, that is just the buyers. It was like the most insane thing ever. And so we did that and it was huge. And what's cool about it is that people that are at the checkout lines, they see this line of people and it's just like insane social proof, you don't get from an app, right? And so we had people, even hours later, who were still buying because they're waiting, they're waiting and they keep seeing people out there like, gosh, I gotta be part of this thing. And so the buying process happened about four hours after the pitch ended, which was awesome. I feel bad for all the other speakers because nobody was in the stadium for the next three or four presentations. We had sucked everybody out, it was amazing. So that was cool. Next piece of it, processing a thousand credit cards in a day is a big process. So we had a whole team set up, we rented a suite, got them all home into Red Bulls, and we had a whole process up there. And so what happened, they were running through as fast as they could processing people's things, right? And half of people's cards fail, like it's the most annoying thing in the world. So like, making failed piles on them. And so we got, everyone processed it, that was successful. And we took everyone who'd failed and we texted them all, like, hey, your thing failed, tomorrow morning at 8 a.m., we're gonna be at the order table. If you want your stuff, you need to get there. And so that pushed people there. And we got super high conversion rates of people coming, getting your credit cards updated there. It's so hard after you leave the event to get credit card declines to work, it's almost impossible, because they're out of the spirit. So you gotta try to do it while you're still there. And so they were texting people like crazy, calling people, and we got the majority of the people, we actually got charged cards, billed everything while we were at the event. The next thing is after the presentation was over, we're talking to Grant and his team, and they're all excited. And I was like, hey, we'll be back on stage tomorrow. And he's like, why am I, I just like, give me 10 minutes. I think I can push a lot more people over the edge. And so we have to understand is like, when we sell stuff normally, right, like the hyperactive buyers you buy initially are like the ones who are emotionally touched and they buy. And then the ones who that night are laying in bed and they're talking to their spouse and thinking about it, the logical buyers are harder to push over typically. So we came back the second day with like a logic close. And so he had me going on stage, I had like five minutes I think, and basically all he does, I made a play to logic, like logically, like this makes sense logically, you guys want this result. The only logical way to get that is by doing this with us right now, right, playing off of logic. And then I made another offer with another hook story offer. I was like, how many of you just saw me make $3 million in last night in 90 minutes yesterday? And they're like, who here wants to learn how I did that? And they're like, oh my gosh, I said, okay, this is the deal for all of you guys who bought yesterday. I'm gonna do a master class showing you guys exactly how I did what I did. I'm gonna fence, and hold my hands up. This is the deal. For any of you guys who buy before, during the next, we had a break right afterwards, during the break, I will let you come to that as well. So technically I stole from him. But yeah, we've got the coolest shot in the world. Anyway, that's awesome. But number two is actually a strategy behind it as well. How many of you guys have seen the documentary Push? No. Okay, let me tell you the hook and the story of Push. So Push is Darren Brown. If you guys have seen Darren Brown's videos, he's a dude who like, who go into bus station and mass hypnotize everybody. You know, say something, I'll pass out. And you're like, what? How did he do that? Anyway, so he did a documentary. The premise of this documentary is like, if I took some human being, could I use social pressure and social proof and in a 90 minute period of time convince them to commit murder? And he takes four people through, takes four people through his experiment, three of the four people commit murder. They've been pushing the person off a building, thinking that they killed the person. Obviously they're on a bungee cord and they don't die. But three of the four people, he gets to commit murder in a 90 minute period of time. And it's all about, oh, like, you will be sick to your stomach the entire time watching it. But you're gonna be like, oh my gosh, if you can convince somebody to commit murder in 90 minutes, what can I do on stage in 90 minutes? I can get them to stand up and go to the back of the room, right? But it's all about, it starts with, you watch it, he's like, we don't start, go push them off a cliff, right? It starts with asking them to do a little thing and then a little bigger thing. And you're asking them to do these little trial clothes, the trial things, all throughout the thing in 90 minutes he's able to escalate it to this experience. And so for me, within two and a half minutes of the presentation, I've gotten every single person in the audience to pull their phone out of their pocket, to light it up, to hold it up. And now they're in rapport with me, because they're mirroring and matching me, right? They're doing all their friends to the next room. Now they have rapport with people that are sitting next to them and rapport with me, and it just like instantly increases the rapport from everybody. Plus they've already, I've commanded them to do something and then they did it. And it's a slippery slope. After you're able to get somebody in that state where you're telling them to do stuff and they're following you, it's hard for them to break out of that. So if you guys want a crazy 90 minute documentary, go watch, push, it's on Netflix, it's crazy. You probably heard a couple of other groups like, I watched it last night, I felt sick to my stomach. You will. And then you'll be excited. Yeah. Can you imagine those people that actually committed murder, how messed up you'd be afterwards? I was thinking, I'm seeing it was like after that, at night we're like, I can't believe it. It's crazy. It's crazy how quick. Yeah, 90 minute, it wasn't like a six month thing, it was like 90 minutes, yeah. One thing that we got lucky enough to do was to see at that 10X event. And I heard Russell talk about this a lot, like the stage rush and all that kind of stuff. So before we went down, I'm like, this is gonna be really interesting because we were down to the IP section. And so James was like, So James was like, okay, it's time to go. So we're like, sweet, let's go. So here's what we're gonna, we're all getting the low down. So we all go down, we're all at the table and sitting there, all of a sudden no one's coming. No one's coming, I'm like, oh geez, it's crickets. Like you saying, I was like, oh, super nervous. Then all of a sudden it was the most unreal thing ever. Like it was just all these people there in just mass and they had their forms and they had anxiety. Like they were ready to just do it. And it was one of those moments that was priceless for me because it was just like, man, it's not just talk, this is reality. Like to see it firsthand happen like that was pretty amazing, like to witness it and see it because you hear about it and I've heard it for years and people talk about it, but to actually witness it, it was, yeah, it was breathtaking to walk. Some things that we kept from, there were some of ours. So I kind of walked through a lot of them. So number one, whole events focused around a result, right? Two karma club. Like that's what we wanted everyone to get and wanted them to believe. Like everything was tied like creating belief in that thing. Okay, now if you look at the, we did it in a four day event because I got excited at three o'clock in the morning one day after we'd already booked hotels and locked in the room and everything, I got excited and wanted to do four days. Well just from the family. I'm just sorry kid. It's just gone from the family. I understand that, yeah. That's our corporate and that's all I want. I'm a million percent understand. Okay, so the way, so a couple of things. Like look at the first day of the event. Again, it's all about creating belief, right? And then also the second piece is like, you don't ever want to be the first person to sell something, right? When I spoke at 10X, I didn't want to be the first person to sell because you have to like train people how to buy. Like that's a big, it takes a lot of effort to train someone how to buy. So 10X I had to do that because they train people how to buy on their phone. So the very first day of our events, if you guys always notice, we've always consistently had a charity. There's a couple reasons. Number one, I actually really care about the charities. Number two, when you introduce a charity, it builds your rapport with your audience instantly because now you're not just the selfish person who's trying to get money. And then number three, it teaches people how to buy. Okay, it's the very first time where I tell people like if you want to donate, you stand up and there's a table back there to go to. So I'm training people to process so that the next day or two days later when we introduce the offer, they know how it all works. I learned that initially from Bill Glazer. They used to do it at their events. They would take, they'd make a huge book of swipe files and the very first day they would have, and they didn't, anyway, I love Bill, but I don't think they really cared as much about the charity because I don't even remember what the charities were. It was never like, it was like, hey, there's some charity and if you want, if you donate any amount of money at the back table, we'll give you the books. They'd stack some books. I remember running back and just like, everyone's throwing money at them. And he told me that there's like, he said my entire goal is to train the audience, how to get out of the chairs and run to the back and give money for something. Now they've done it once, now they know how to do it. So whenever I speak at a multi-speaker event, I don't want to be the first person selling. I want somebody else to cut their teeth and train the audience on how to buy. So when I come in, that's already been done. I can just push people to the table and it's really, really easy. So that's kind of in a multi, your own multi-day event, the charity aspect, that's one of the big reasons is it trains people on how to get up and how to go to actually give you money. All right, that's number one. Day number two, which is three for us, but then the second day of the event is again, all about belief. And so obviously a lot of speakers who've already gotten into Comic Club, sharing their process, how they did it, those type of things. And then when I speak at a live event, like this type of event, I like personally speaking day two before lunch, like that's my sweet spot I always fight for. So like selling, and then be able to close all through lunch and on. And it's hard to get people back from lunch afterwards. And so, but at a live event, it's a little bit different because the buying process is more extended. So I don't wanna like sell too early and then people have, this is something we got from Sage. You don't wanna have people have a whole bunch of time to like get back in other presentations and get re-excited and remotivated. Like after you make the presentation, you wanna have kind of a drop in energy where people who didn't buy just feel this nervous uneasiness for a long time, okay? So we had lunch on the second day or the third day for us. And then you know it's after lunch, getting people back to heart. So we do the ward ceremony right there, just showing more social proof. Every single person coming on stage, coming on stage, seeing the results, seeing the results, seeing the results, seeing the results, over and over and over and over and over and over again, right? Because the more they have to see the result, the more they wanna believe. The more they believe they can actually do it, right? And what's insane, this still blows our mind. Last year, it was the first year we did it, this year, the second year. I thought people were gonna be in the hallways and talking and all sorts of stuff. Did you guys notice when we do the ward ceremony? It's like crickets. Nobody moves, it was insane, both years. Even this year it was even longer. Next year we have like five or 600 people getting it. I'm like, it could be a two day event just handing out awards. But nobody leaves, everyone sits there and there's like, I want, that's gonna be me. That's the result, that's gonna be me. I can tell you how many people have told me next year, next year, I'm gonna be on stage with you. Which is awesome, right? And then we took a break afterwards because there's kind of a low on energy after the awards thing for us. So we had a break, reset, we had Sunny D come out, try to get some energy, come back up. And then we came back in and that's when we did the presentation that's focused on the result. Presentation's title was 12 Month Millionaire, how to get the two comic club in the next 12 months. Big focus on the result. And then here's how we're gonna get the result, right? So I did my typical perfect webinar with a couple ninja things, but nothing new or magical there, right? The big difference that Sage kind of brought is how we structured the offer. So they've been working in the back of the room for, who knows, 15, 20 years, right? And they said the biggest problem that people have when they run the back of the room is like, okay, I want this, can I put on two different cars? Can I finance it, do I need to call my mom? I want to do, I think I want to do full pay, but I need to call my bank, I need it. And like all this stuff. And usually it's like the fastest people in the back that have the questions. And so they're in the back, they're like monetizing all the time, the people, everyone else who just wants to buy is in the back waiting, waiting, and they just get frustrated and leave. And so they've been testing a whole bunch of stuff over the last couple decades they've been doing it. The biggest thing they found is like, to get rid of all those kinds of questions is, you guys see how we did it? Like there was a card, people broke it off, it was $800 non-refundable deposit. You go back there, just do that, and then tomorrow at the luncheon, and then we'll figure out, you can do payment plan, you can call your spouse, and you call the bank. We'll figure it out tomorrow. All you gotta do is do $800 down payment right now, non-refundable, and then that means you're in. And so that takes away all of the questions of like the slow down progress of the table. That people just fill it out, run it back, hand it in, and then they're done. Okay, so that was a big piece of it. Obviously as you guys saw, we did the picture thing afterwards, again which worked really well. I sat out there for like two hours doing pictures, so everyone who didn't buy is seeing social proof of whatever he did buy, and sales just kept coming in for a long, long time. Then that night everyone went to dinner, begin, notice we didn't hype the energy back up afterwards. Then they came back, and then we had the coaches who actually were working with them do like, do what do you call it, like live coaching, hot seats and things like that, at kind of a lower energy level, because you want that nervous tension, like should I be doing this, should I not be doing this, like the people who are kind of on the edge, you don't want to give them the freedom of like the next exciting thing that they're gonna be learning about, right? You're gonna keep them in kind of that state, and then they go to bed that night with like that question, should I do it, should I do it, and all those kind of things. Then come back the next morning, the last day, we're lucky enough to have Brian come and talk about search sense of urgency, which was amazing, and kind of set the tone for the day right there, and then afterwards we did what we call, what's called the repitch. And so this comes back, same thing we did at 10X, was like the emotional buyers bought initially, now we gotta talk to the, like we have to get into logical, and like push the other people over the edge. And so I was planning to do a whole presentation, and then luckily for me, like I ran out of time doing my slides, and I was stressing out about that presentation, and I was like, you know what would be cool? Like what if Myron could come up, and like just tell stories with me, and we'd close people like that. Initially it was gonna be like me and him doing a bunch of stories, and then like, we decided to have Myron do it, and he was totally cool doing it, and it was the most amazing, it was like the most amazing that he has ever seen ever. So he's now the king of the repitch, but basically came back over and, yeah I don't know the exact numbers, but it was probably another two and a half, three million or more in sales that he pushed over with the repitch, getting on, I don't even know, I told you, it's like friends of mine who have been getting free stuff from me their entire lives got out of their butt after you spoke and bought. I was like one of my cheapest friends, I love him to death, cheapest person ever. First thing he's ever bought for me ever, was that because Myron sold them, I was amazing. Do you wanna comment on any of like your magic? Sure, because it was magic. I'm having a time to say what. So really a big part of selling from the stage, one of the biggest parts of it is expectation, and my expectation comes right now from the fact that I've done it so many times, so I kinda know what to expect, and you have to fight against allowing yourself to believe in a result that you don't desire. If you do that, what's gonna happen is, you're gonna project that lack of belief onto the people that you're selling to, and the thing that you desire to get them to do is to believe. So if you don't believe, you're making them not believe, and I know that sounds hocus pocus, but it's not, it's how it really works. And the other thing is, obviously Russell had a great offer, so that helps. I mean the offer was so good, I said, I'm doing this, right? So I know if I'm doing it, I mean it just makes sense. And the other thing is, like when I got to the part about the two biggest expenses, a lot of people don't realize that when you're selling something, you're not just selling something, you're always selling against something. If you don't tell them what you're selling against, if you don't tell them what the cost is of not buying, they're gonna think that not buying is, well if I don't buy this, then I can go buy something else that's cheaper. You have to show them that if they don't buy this, they're actually paying way more. So I positioned the $18,000 offer beside the $10 million that they weren't gonna make if they didn't buy it. I mean it's good math, it's even better logic, and the psychology is just so good, right? So good. That, and I guess the other thing, and I think that anybody who's, even if everything else stays the same, if you do this one thing, it'll multiply your sales numbers in front of a room, and it gives you the ability to do something in front of a room that you can't do on a webinar. And that's what I call choir director closing. Like I used to be a choir director, what does a choir director do? He gets everybody singing in harmony, so everybody's singing the same thing. So one of the keys, one of the key things that I do when I'm speaking in front of a room is if I ask a question, I will give them the answer that I want all of them to give back to me. In other words, I don't say, does that make sense to everybody? Because some people say yes, some people say yes sir, some people say mm-hmm, some people say uh-huh, right? So I'll say, does that make sense to everyone say yes? Yes. Oh, that was weak. Maybe you didn't understand my question. Does that make sense to everyone say yes? Yes. And then that's what happens. And so now every time I ask a question, I'll get everybody to answer me. If that makes sense to you, say that makes sense. Yes. And I raise my hand, and I get them raising their hand more like that micro commitment stuff you were talking about. I call it choir director closing. I don't let everybody give me the answer they wanna give me, I make them give me the answer I want. Here's why. Everybody wants to do what everybody's doing. Nobody wants to do it, nobody's doing it. So if I get everybody saying yes, then the people who are sitting beside you and around you and in front of you and in back of you are helping you make the decision that you already wanna make. Notice, you wanna make it. I want you to make it too, but you wanna make it. So I'm getting them to help you make the decision you already wanna make. Like if you do that one thing, if you will give people the answers that you desire them to give back to you, so everybody answers you in harmony. Answers you in what? Harmony. Harmony. Then you'll double your sales just by doing that one thing. Dang. Give mine a round of applause. Stop. That is amazing. Who's here ever heard Tony Robbins say? Say aye. Say aye. You're saying that I'm like, ah, it's amazing. And I think, I don't know if we're gonna do this part next. Like the way that Sage does theirs, they do the commitment card and the next day they do lunch, then lunch they pick the payment plans, all kind of stuff. Our problem is we were expecting that 250 people, thanks to Myron, we had like 650 people. So it was like, it was kind of nuts and chaotic and way harder than we thought it was gonna be to like execute on that. Cause that's like figure out the lunch plans. And so I think if you're doing a smaller group, the way that they, the big thing they wanted the lunch to be like, we're gonna start training right now because as soon as you start training, people are less likely to leave because they're part of the whole process. Because of just the math of how it worked out, we weren't able to do that part of it. But in a smaller group, that's what happens. You come there at the thing, you start the actual training and it's very sumptuous like, hey everyone, here's the next form picked if you're doing monthly payment or if you're doing yearly. And if you choose monthly, if you don't know choose monthly and you've got 60 days, you can call us and upgrade to the other thing later. So that way there's no one like, ah, I talked to, it's just like, you pick which one of the options you want. Also because they lunch through, there's reciprocity, there's less likely they're gonna cancel. Cause in fact, we had one guy, apparently he went with somebody and then he had, he came and ate lunch and his partners backed out on him. GSC's in Facebook, his whole post like, I'm so sorry I screwed Russell, I feel so bad. Like, I'm gonna like pay him back for lunch and like all this stuff. People feel sick to their stomach if they've eaten, if you've broken bread with them and then you're asking for the money back. And so that's kind of the strategy of the lunch part that happened afterwards. And then, yeah, so those are the core things, yeah.
